questions.scenario: DevPolaris stores short-lived checkout reservation items in DynamoDB. Each item has `expiresAt` set two hours after creation, but the product team requires the API to reject expired reservations immediately and also publish downstream cleanup events when DynamoDB eventually removes old items. Which design is correct?

options.text: Rely on TTL deletion time as the API's authorization check because DynamoDB removes an item exactly at `expiresAt`.
Check `expiresAt` in the application for immediate behavior, enable TTL for eventual cleanup, and consume DynamoDB Streams if downstream processing needs removal events.
Use a global secondary index on `expiresAt` and scan it before each checkout because TTL cannot coexist with Streams.
Delete expired items synchronously from every read request so TTL and stream records are unnecessary.

questions.explanation:
DynamoDB TTL is asynchronous, so application behavior that must change at the exact expiry time should read the timestamp itself. TTL can clean up later, and DynamoDB Streams can support downstream processing of service deletions when designed for that event. A scan on every checkout is not an exact-key access pattern, and synchronous deletion makes the request path own cleanup work it does not need.

Learn more in [DynamoDB TTL](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazondynamodb/latest/developerguide/TTL.html) and [DynamoDB Streams](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazondynamodb/latest/developerguide/Streams.html).

questions.scenario:
A retry uses the same idempotency key but a different request hash:

```text
idempotencyKey=checkout_01HW2S8RN8Z7
existingRequestHash=sha256:18f9b31c
incomingRequestHash=sha256:bead9021
```

What should the app do?

options.text: Overwrite the item because the new hash is more recent.
Create a second order and keep both hashes under the same key.
Delete the existing item so the client can try again with no history.
Reject the request as key reuse for different input.

questions.explanation:
An idempotency key is a promise that retries represent the same operation. A different request hash means the client reused the key for different input. Rejecting it protects checkout from mixing two logical requests.

Learn more in [DynamoDB condition expressions](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazondynamodb/latest/developerguide/Expressions.ConditionExpressions.html).

questions.scenario:
CloudWatch shows throttling concentrated on one partition key:

```text
pk=JOB writeRequestsPerMinute=92000
pk=JOB#job_01HW2S8T23Q1 writeRequestsPerMinute=3
pk=JOB#job_01HW2S9A77Q writeRequestsPerMinute=2
```

Which key design is the likely problem?

options.text: Many writes are using the same broad partition key value `JOB` instead of spreading by real job id.
Increase table-wide write capacity first because concentrated traffic will then distribute across more partition-key values.
Add a GSI on the same low-cardinality status value so the write path has another index to absorb the hotspot.
Reduce item size because smaller writes remove the need to address a single hot partition key.

questions.explanation:
A hot key happens when one partition key receives much more traffic than others. `pk=JOB` piles many jobs onto one value, while `JOB#{jobId}` spreads work by the id the app already has. Type prefixes are fine when the rest of the key includes real identity.

Learn more in [DynamoDB partition key design](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazondynamodb/latest/developerguide/bp-partition-key-design.html).

questions.scenario:
The team is reviewing the idempotency item shape:

```json
{"pk":"IDEMPOTENCY#checkout_01HW2S8RN8Z7","sk":"REQUEST","requestHash":"sha256:18f9b31c","status":"processing","jobId":"job_01HW2W7Z8MP4","expiresAt":1777713102}
```

Which review comments are useful?

options.text: The `requestHash` helps detect key reuse with different checkout input.
The item should omit expiration planning because idempotency records must stay forever.
The key includes a clear type prefix so logs and reads can build the same exact key.
The status should guide the duplicate response, such as still processing or already succeeded.
Use a scan filtered by idempotency key during retries so the table can keep a random partition-key design.

questions.explanation:
The key prefix, request hash, and status all support safe retry behavior. Expiration planning is also important because idempotency records are operational state, not permanent order history. The retention window should match the retry and support need.

Learn more in [DynamoDB time to live](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazondynamodb/latest/developerguide/TTL.html).

questions.scenario: DevPolaris is designing a DynamoDB table for checkout idempotency and background-job status. The API must claim a request by exact idempotency key, retrieve the existing result for a retry, list jobs for one customer by creation time, and update a job by its job ID. The team has not chosen partition or sort keys yet. What should drive the table design first?

options.text: Choose a high-cardinality partition key first, then add scans when the required customer and idempotency lookups do not match it.
Turn each known question into a partition key and optional sort key shape.
Use a random UUID partition key for every item, then add scans for customer and idempotency lookups.
Use `customerId` alone as the partition key for all records because grouping customer history is the first stated query.

questions.explanation:
DynamoDB design starts with the reads and writes the application must perform. Each access pattern should map to a key the app already has. Starting from every future filter is more like SQL thinking and can lead to table scans.

Learn more in [DynamoDB NoSQL design](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazondynamodb/latest/developerguide/bp-general-nosql-design.html).

questions.scenario:
The duplicate request logs:

```text
awsError=ConditionalCheckFailedException
idempotencyKey=checkout_01HW2S8RN8Z7
requestHash=sha256:18f9b31c
existingStatus=succeeded
existingOrderId=ord_1042
```

What should the API usually return?

options.text: Write a new item for every retry and reconcile duplicate orders in a scheduled cleanup job.
Use a conditional put, but return a generic conflict without reading the existing operation status.
Use a strongly consistent read before an unconditional write because the read prevents another writer from racing.
The existing successful result for `ord_1042`, because the same logical request already completed.

questions.explanation:
Same key, same request hash, and succeeded status are exactly the case idempotency is meant to handle. Returning the existing result makes the retry safe for the customer. Treating it as an outage or creating a new order would defeat the design.

Learn more in [DynamoDB condition expressions](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazondynamodb/latest/developerguide/Expressions.ConditionExpressions.html).

questions.scenario:
A checkout lookup misses even though the write log shows an item was created:

```text
write pk=IDEMPOTENCY#checkout_01HY8F4Z9R7JQ2K6P0 sk=REQUEST
read  pk=checkout_01HY8F4Z9R7JQ2K6P0 sk=REQUEST result=MISS
```

What is the best diagnosis?

options.text: The read key forgot the `IDEMPOTENCY#` prefix, so the app is asking for a different item.
The base-table read needs a global secondary index before it can use the partition and sort key shown in the write log.
The table needs an RDS foreign key to keep prefixes attached.
S3 lifecycle expired the DynamoDB item before the read.

questions.explanation:
The write and read keys are not the same. DynamoDB looks up the exact key values the app provides. Shared key-building helpers can prevent this class of bug.

Learn more in [DynamoDB partition key design](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazondynamodb/latest/developerguide/bp-partition-key-design.html).
